title: Cat Head Tilt and Falling Over: Causes and Emergency Care
meta_desc: If your cat has a head tilt and is falling over, it may be vestibular disease. Learn why this is a high-priority emergency and how to seek help.
content_html:
TL;DR: A sudden head tilt and loss of balance in cats is a high-priority emergency involving the vestibular system that requires immediate veterinary evaluation. Early intervention is essential to stabilize your cat and rule out life-threatening conditions like strokes or toxins.

What causes a cat to suddenly tilt its head and fall over?

When a cat suddenly develops a head tilt and begins falling over, it typically indicates a problem with the vestibular system. This system is responsible for maintaining balance and orientation. Disruption to this system causes a sensation of extreme vertigo, making it difficult for your cat to stand or walk straight.

Should I take my cat to the emergency vet for a sudden head tilt?

  • Urgency Level: High. This should be treated as a medical emergency.
  • Symptoms can mirror life-threatening conditions such as a stroke, brain inflammation, or poisoning.
  • Other potential causes include inner ear infections or "idiopathic" vestibular disease.
  • Because your cat is likely disoriented and distressed, immediate veterinary intervention is required to provide stabilization and find the underlying cause.

How can a photo or video help the vet triage my cat’s balance issues?

  • Capture a short video of your cat's behavior and movement to provide vital information to the triage team.
  • Take a clear photo of their eyes so veterinarians can check for involuntary eye movements known as nystagmus.
  • Documenting the specific direction of the head tilt helps the medical team assess the severity of neurological signs.
  • Providing this evidence allows the hospital to prioritize your cat's care accurately upon arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, head tilt is indicative of vestibular system disease, involving cranial nerve VIII, the rostral medulla oblongata, or the cerebellum. Unilateral or asymmetric vestibular lesions manifest as head tilt, dysequilibrium, and a tendency to circle, fall, or roll towards one side. Examination should assess for spontaneous nystagmus, both with the head in a normal position and in deviated positions, and for abnormal eye position (ventral strabismus) when the nose is elevated. Unilateral lesions of the vestibulocochlear nerve often produce dysequilibrium with a head tilt toward the side of the lesion, accompanied by spontaneous positional, horizontal, or rotary nystagmus. Bilateral vestibular lesions result in dysequilibrium, wide side-to-side head excursions (often without head tilt), and loss of normal vestibular nystagmus. Rarely, a cerebellar or cerebellar peduncle lesion can cause a head tilt away from the lesion (paradoxical head tilt), accompanied by ipsilateral conscious proprioceptive deficits, hemiparesis, and hypermetria of the limbs on the same side.

Chapter: Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1214)

faqs.question: Can an ear infection cause my cat to fall over?
faqs.answer: Yes, a severe infection in the inner or middle ear can disrupt the vestibular system, leading to a loss of balance and a persistent head tilt.

faqs.question: What should I do while transporting my cat to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your cat in a well-padded carrier to prevent them from injuring themselves while stumbling. Keep the environment quiet and dark to reduce stress and nausea.

faqs.question: Is a head tilt always permanent?
faqs.answer: In many cases, the head tilt improves as the underlying condition is treated, though some cats may retain a slight, permanent tilt even after recovering their balance.
